frontmatter.title: Why I Built This Site the Way I Did
frontmatter.description: The reasoning behind felipetavares.dev: Astro islands, hexagonal ports, a $0 stack forced by a hard constraint, and what I'd do differently now that more of it is shipped.

articles.body:
Most personal sites start with a template and end with a WordPress plugin problem. I wanted the
opposite: a site whose own construction is a proof point for the kind of architecture leadership
I write about. This is the story of the decisions behind felipetavares.dev — not a features list,
but the reasoning, including the parts I got wrong or deferred.

## The brief I gave myself

The site had one non-negotiable constraint: **$0/month recurring**, domain already owned. Everything
else — the CMS, the chatbot, the hosting, the analytics — had to fit inside free tiers with enough
headroom that a personal site would never bump into a limit. That constraint did more design work
than any aesthetic preference. It ruled out a hosted CMS with seat pricing, ruled out a
pay-per-request LLM API as the default path, and pointed hard at a platform that already gives
generous free compute: Cloudflare.

It also ruled out over-engineering. A personal brand site doesn't need a database, doesn't need
auth, doesn't need a search cluster. The interesting problem here isn't scale — it's making a
content-heavy, SEO-critical, occasionally-interactive site that stays fast, stays cheap, and stays
easy to extend without a rewrite six months in.

## Astro, and why islands over a full SPA

The site is built on **Astro 5** in static-first mode, with React used only for islands —
components that genuinely need client-side interactivity: the theme toggle, the mobile menu, the
contact form, the chat widget. Everything else — hero copy, navigation, article bodies, the
projects grid — ships as plain HTML with zero client JavaScript.

This matters for a site whose primary audience is a recruiter or a peer technologist skimming on
a phone during a commute. Shipping a full React SPA for mostly-static content means paying a
hydration tax on every page for interactivity nobody asked for. Astro's islands architecture lets
me keep that JavaScript budget close to zero on content pages and spend it only where it earns its
keep — a rule the design spec states explicitly as a performance budget (total JS on content pages
under 50KB, islands lazy-loaded).

The Cloudflare adapter runs the same codebase in **hybrid output**: pages are prerendered at build
time onto the CDN, and the handful of routes that genuinely need a server — `/api/contact`,
`/api/chat` — run as Workers functions. One deployment target, two rendering modes, chosen per
route.

## Hexagonal architecture for a site with "no backend"

The part that surprises people: this site has a `src/core/` directory with zero framework imports,
organized as pure TypeScript entities, ports, and services — the same hexagonal (ports & adapters)
discipline I'd use on a much larger system. For a marketing site, that can look like
over-engineering. It isn't, for one specific reason: **the content source is a stated architecture
decision, not a foregone one.**

Content lives in git today — Astro content collections reading MDX and JSON under `/content` —
but the design spec calls this out as adapter #1 of several possible ones, with a hosted CMS
(Sanity, Payload) as a plausible future adapter if the editing experience ever needs to outgrow
git. The `ContentRepository` port is the seam:

```ts
export interface ContentRepository {
  getArticles(opts?: { includeDrafts?: boolean }): Promise<Article[]>;
  getArticle(slug: string): Promise<Article | undefined>;
  getProjects(): Promise<Project[]>;
  // ...roles, talks, testimonials, site singletons
}
```

Every page imports `contentRepository` from `src/config/site.ts` — never the adapter directly.
`GitContentRepository` is the only class that knows content lives in Astro collections. Swapping
that adapter for a hosted CMS later is a new file plus a one-line change in `config/site.ts`; zero
page-level changes. The same pattern applies to the chatbot's LLM provider — Workers AI today,
Claude or another provider via a config-driven router rather than hardcoded into a component.

Is this the simplest possible thing that could work? No — a page that calls `getCollection()`
directly would be simpler for exactly one content source. But I've built and rebuilt career-tooling
systems enough times to know that the "just call the API directly" version is the one that gets
expensive to change. The port costs a small amount of indirection up front and buys back an
afternoon, not a rewrite, when the content backend changes.

## A design that degrades instead of breaking

The visual language is deliberately layered so it degrades rather than breaking. Glass cards use
`backdrop-filter: blur() saturate()` with a `@supports` fallback to a solid surface. The
site-wide galaxy backdrop (WebGL, React Bits Galaxy.tsx under an MIT license) is gated behind
motion preferences and renders near-invisible in light mode — fine ink specks instead of glowing
stars — so content stays dominant. The hero (a vendored WebGL brain canvas) carries the site's
own content, not placeholder copy, so it's readable even if the animation never fires.

One design token source (`src/styles/tokens.css`) drives both dark and light themes through CSS
custom properties, switched by a single class on `<html>`. Tailwind v4's `@theme` block maps
those tokens into utility classes, so component markup never hardcodes a hex value — change a
token, both themes update everywhere at once.

The principle: no visual feature is load-bearing for content or navigation. That's the actual
accessibility contract, not an afterthought applied after the fact.

## What this article demonstrates

This article is a small integration test of the publishing pipeline: `/writing` lists it by tag
and reading time, this page renders a table of contents from its headings, and the publish-kit
script (`pnpm publish-kit <slug>`) can turn this single MDX file into a Substack-ready HTML
export, a LinkedIn post, a Reddit framing note, and an X thread — without hand-copying content
between five editors. Write once in the canonical source, syndicate outward through generated
artifacts rather than manual rewrites.

## What I'd reconsider

Two trade-offs I'd revisit. First, Keystatic runs in local mode in dev only for now —
GitHub-mode editing (so I can publish from a phone without a terminal) needs a GitHub App I
haven't set up yet. The git workflow is fast enough that this hasn't blocked anything, but it's
the gap between "works" and "works from anywhere."

Second, I underestimated how fast the design would evolve. The initial architectural decisions —
Astro islands, hexagonal core, $0 Cloudflare stack — held up well across multiple design phases.
But I spent time describing a design (liquid glass, gradient mesh hero) that got replaced before
the site shipped publicly. The architecture was the right thing to pin in writing early; the
visual identity less so.

The chatbot is live and uses the same `core/ports` pattern I planned for it. That part went
exactly as intended.

frontmatter.title: felipetavares.dev
frontmatter.tagline: This site, engineered like a product — not a template.
frontmatter.northStar: Proof that enterprise architectural discipline holds at personal-brand scale — built and shipped at $0/month.
frontmatter.problem: Personal-brand sites default to templates or paid CMSes. This one had to read as engineered, with $0/month in recurring costs and no vendor lock-in.
outcomes: $0/month recurring cost, running entirely on Cloudflare's free tier
Three equivalent content-editing paths — file, Claude Code, or a browser UI — no seat-priced CMS
Redaction gate in CI: no employer-tied financials ever reach the public site

problem.summary: A personal-brand site with the same discipline as an enterprise system — on a $0 budget.
problem.detail: The brief: build a personal-brand site that reads as engineered, not templated — with a hard $0/month recurring-cost constraint, domain already owned. That ruled out a seat-priced hosted CMS and a pay-per-request LLM API as defaults.

approach.summary: Hexagonal ports & adapters underneath, Astro islands on top.
approach.detail: src/core/ is pure TypeScript with zero framework or provider imports, organized as hexagonal ports & adapters (ContentRepository, LlmProvider, EmbeddingIndex, EmailSender, RateLimiter). React is used only for islands that genuinely need client-side interactivity, so content pages ship close to zero client JS.

architecture.summary: Astro 5 hybrid output on Cloudflare — prerendered pages, Workers for the rest.
architecture.detail: Pages prerender to the CDN at build time; the handful of routes that need a server (/api/contact, /api/chat) run as Workers functions. Cloudflare's free tier supplies Pages/Workers hosting, KV, Vectorize, and Workers AI as the primary LLM provider, all inside headroom a personal site won't bump into.

results.summary: $0/month recurring, 3 equivalent content-editing paths, sub-50KB content-page JS.
results.detail: Content is git-backed Astro content collections edited through three equivalent paths — direct file edit, Claude Code, or the Keystatic UI. A redaction gate enforces no employer-tied financial figures or headcounts anywhere on the public site, scanned in CI on every commit.

projects.body:
felipetavares.dev is one of two proof points on this site, alongside Selfwright: the same
architectural discipline an enterprise system demands — ports & adapters, a hard cost constraint,
a content pipeline with an enforced redaction rule — applied at personal-brand scale. The cards
above give the shape; what follows is the full picture.

## Architecture

The site runs on **Astro 5** in hybrid output on the **Cloudflare** adapter — pages prerender to
the CDN at build time; the handful of routes that need a server (`/api/contact`, `/api/chat`) run
as Workers functions. React is used only for **islands** — components that genuinely need
client-side interactivity (theme toggle, mobile menu, contact form, chat widget, accessibility
panel) — so content pages ship close to zero client JS.

Underneath, `src/core/` is pure TypeScript with zero framework or provider imports, organized as
**hexagonal ports & adapters**: `ContentRepository`, `LlmProvider`, `EmbeddingIndex`,
`EmailSender`, `RateLimiter`. Every page and API route imports through the port's single binding
point (`src/config/site.ts`, `src/config/llm.ts`) — never an adapter directly. Swapping git for
a hosted CMS, or Workers AI for a different model provider, is a new adapter file and a one-line
config change, not a page-level rewrite.

## The $0 stack

The one non-negotiable constraint was **$0/month recurring**, domain already owned. That ruled out
a seat-priced hosted CMS and a pay-per-request LLM API as the default path, and pointed at
Cloudflare's free tier: Pages/Workers hosting, KV for rate limiting, Vectorize for embeddings, and
**Workers AI as the primary LLM provider** (with Anthropic's API as an optional, config-swappable
fallback) — all inside free-tier headroom a personal site won't bump into. Contact form email delivery
runs through **Resend** (REST API, key stored as a Worker env binding). Bot protection on `/api/contact`
uses **Cloudflare Turnstile** — server-side verification, sitekey public, secret stays in the Worker.

## Git-based CMS, three editing modes

Content is git-backed Astro content collections (MDX for articles/projects, JSON for roles/talks/
site singletons) — one content model, edited through three equivalent paths: a direct file edit,
Claude Code, or the **Keystatic** UI at `/keystatic` in dev (`storage.kind: 'local'` for now;
GitHub-mode storage is a documented TODO pending a GitHub App). `astro.config.mjs` only registers
the Keystatic integration outside `astro build`, so no admin routes exist in the deployed site.

## Design system

The site uses glass-card surfaces throughout (`backdrop-filter: blur() saturate()` with solid
fallbacks), a token-first theming layer (one CSS file drives both dark and light themes through
custom properties), and a Deep Signal hero — a vendored WebGL brain canvas that carries the
site's own content (kinetic type, headline, CTAs) rather than placeholder copy. The galaxy
backdrop (site-wide WebGL layer) sits below all page content and adapts to light and dark themes,
scaling down to fine ink-speck density in light mode so content stays dominant.

One design token source (`src/styles/tokens.css`) drives both themes through CSS custom
properties switched by a single class on `<html>`. Tailwind v4's `@theme` block maps those
tokens into utility classes, so no component hardcodes a hex value.

## Redaction-gated content pipeline

A hard content rule — no employer-tied financial figures or headcounts anywhere on the public
site — is enforced editorially across role JSONs, the dossier, the home credibility strip,
project MDX, and the chatbot's index. Metrics either use relative multiples ("cut integration
cost by more than half"), magnitude bands ("8-figure portfolio"), or public non-financial facts.
The chatbot's index is rebuilt after every redaction pass so it can't surface a pre-redaction
figure from a stale chunk.

## RAG chatbot with a provider router

`/api/chat` streams answers grounded in this site's own content via Server-Sent Events. The
retrieval index is built by `pnpm build-chat-index` (`scripts/build-chat-index.ts`), chunking
articles, projects, roles, and site copy. `LlmRouter` tries Workers AI first and falls back to
Anthropic without any component-level change. The chat launcher is a 56px avatar circle (the
owner's raster brand glyph) with a green presence dot — no persistent server state, no PII
storage.

## The publish-kit

`pnpm publish-kit <slug>` turns one canonical MDX article into four syndication artifacts —
Substack HTML, a LinkedIn post, a Reddit framing note, an X thread — so writing is authored once
and syndicated outward rather than rewritten per platform. Dev.to and Hashnode pull the RSS feed
directly.

## The /reading section

`/reading` is a public log of books, articles, and papers with the owner's verdict on each —
one way his judgment compounds in public rather than staying in private notes. Each entry carries
a type, domains, and a verdict; filter chips let readers narrow by either axis.

## Decisions

**Astro over Next.js.** The site is mostly static, SEO-critical content read on commutes. Astro's
islands architecture ships near-zero client JS on content pages by default; a Next.js app would
have meant fighting its client-first defaults for a site that doesn't need app-router-grade
client state.

**Git-based CMS over Sanity/Payload from day one.** A hosted CMS adds a seat-priced dependency
and a second source of truth before there's any evidence the git + Keystatic workflow can't keep
up with actual publishing cadence. The `ContentRepository` port keeps that door open without
paying for it now.

**Workers AI first, Anthropic as fallback — not the reverse.** Workers AI is free within
Cloudflare's tier and colocates with the rest of the stack; routing through it first keeps the
chatbot inside the $0 budget for normal traffic, with a paid-API fallback available via config
if quality or availability demands it.

**Flip page-transition morphs skipped, in favor of stability.** The design spec included Astro
View Transitions-driven Flip morphs between card and hero states. Landing the higher-value items
— Lenis, section reveals, the accessibility control center, card magnetics — cleanly took
priority; Flip remains a documented option.

**The galaxy backdrop as a deliberate, logged budget exception.** The site-wide WebGL galaxy
layer (React Bits Galaxy.tsx, MIT) adds motion-conditional render overhead. It's gated behind
`motionEnabled() && fx !== 'off'`, scales to low-density on mobile, and near-disappears in light
mode. The trade-off is logged; a config flag drops it instantly if needed.

<Callout title="More decisions, the full stack, a changelog">
This page covers the shape of the build. `/engineering` goes further: six more decision records
rewritten executive-readable, every piece of the stack with its one honest reason, and a dated
changelog distilled from this repo's real commit history — no marketing copy about engineering
discipline, the discipline itself. [Read the engineering hub →](/engineering)
</Callout>

## What this demonstrates

The same discipline used on enterprise systems — ports before adapters, a $0-forced architecture
rather than a padded one, redaction as a hard content gate, a design system with one signature
device — holds up on a constrained, real, shipped system. The decisions, stack rationale, and a
dated changelog are on [/engineering](/engineering).

The reasoning behind the architectural decisions is also written up as an article: [Why I Built
This Site the Way I Did](/writing/why-i-built-this-site-the-way-i-did).

## Status

Active — this is the live, deployed site you're reading. It ships in phases (documented in the
addendum's delivery table), each gated on `pnpm check`, `pnpm test`, `pnpm build`, and a
Lighthouse/axe pass before merge.

frontmatter.title: Selfwright
frontmatter.tagline: An open-core, local-first career operating system: every output traces to a locked evidence registry.
frontmatter.northStar: A career operating system built on one constraint: every output traces back to verified evidence.
frontmatter.problem: Career tools optimize for keyword volume. Selfwright starts from a locked evidence registry and produces only what it can verify, then stores every decision made along the way.
outcomes: Every artifact — cover letters, prep packs, drills — passes a truth validator before it can be used
Co-piloted generation with no API keys: the prompt runs in your existing Claude session; no per-call billing
Evidence compounds across applications, interviews, and debriefs; no application starts from scratch
stack: Claude (Anthropic) via a co-piloted generation path

problem.summary: Career tools optimize for volume and keyword-fit, not truth.
problem.detail: Most career and job-search tools optimize for volume and keyword-fit. Selfwright optimizes for truthful, defensible, top-tier output, then compounds everything learned — evidence, stories, decisions, contacts, lessons — into a personal knowledge system.

approach.summary: A locked truth layer, honesty walls, and scored drifts as the moat.
approach.detail: Four compounding goals, in build order: a career engine (truthful, ATS-tuned, archetype-tailored CVs and covers, auto-discovered roles), a coach (interview and networking prep, deliberate skill-gap closing), content (thought-leadership writing support), and expertise (becoming a functional and technical expert across the spaces it touches).

architecture.summary: Hexagonal, DDD, modular monolith — TypeScript-first, zero framework imports in the domain core.
architecture.detail: The domain core is pure TypeScript with zero framework, provider, or storage imports — everything external (LLM calls, storage, memory, rendering, notifications) is reached only through a port, implemented by a swappable adapter. The git-based truth layer is the state of record; a PostgreSQL projection (with pgvector) provides a rebuildable index — never the source of truth itself.

results.summary: Truth validator passes on every generated artifact; evidence base compounds with each interview cycle.
results.detail: Selfwright's fitness-function suite catches architectural drift and truth violations automatically. The truth-trace validator gates every generated document before use. After each interview, the coaching loop feeds debriefs, gap-scans, and drills back into the same evidence base.

projects.body:
Selfwright is an open-core career operating system built around one constraint: every output traces to a locked evidence registry. It is released at 0.6.0 and in active use for career development, built in public under Apache 2.0.

[Read the full overview at /selfwright/](/selfwright/)

roles.title: Senior Product Manager, ERP & Treasury
roles.arc: Designing a trading business from the middle office out
roles.arcPt: Desenhando um negócio de trading a partir do middle office

impact.narrative: Led the team that maintained and improved the bank's SAP ERP, supporting back-office operations across trading and asset management.
impact.narrativePt: Liderou a equipe responsável pela manutenção e melhoria do SAP ERP do banco, apoiando as operações de back-office em trading e gestão de ativos.

impact.narrative: Designed the middle- and back-office process and flows — risk, P&L, position, MTM through settlement — for a commodities trading business stand-up.
impact.narrativePt: Desenhou os processos e fluxos de middle e back office — risco, P&L, posição, MTM até liquidação — para a estruturação de um negócio de trading de commodities.

impact.narrative: Led the implementation of major regulatory programs, coordinating cross-functional squads across the trading and asset-management back office.
impact.narrativePt: Liderou a implementação de grandes programas regulatórios, coordenando squads multifuncionais no back office de trading e gestão de ativos.

roles.title: Global Product Data Leader
roles.arc: Commercializing data as a product and mapping the path to a modernized platform
roles.arcPt: Transformando dados em produto e traçando o caminho para uma plataforma modernizada

impact.narrative: Delivered data products for operational and analytical use cases, heavily increasing revenue across the trading business.
impact.narrativePt: Entregou produtos de dados para casos de uso operacionais e analíticos, aumentando significativamente a receita do negócio de trading.

impact.narrative: Conceived and delivered the Future State Strategy for Data — the blueprint that led to an 8-figure program to modernize the data platform for Agriculture & Trading.
impact.narrativePt: Concebeu e entregou a Future State Strategy para Dados — o blueprint que resultou em um programa de oito dígitos para modernizar a plataforma de dados do Agriculture & Trading.

impact.narrative: Reduced cost across the product-data estate while managing vendor contracts and initiatives to drive efficiency.
impact.narrativePt: Reduziu custos em todo o portfólio de dados de produto, gerenciando contratos e iniciativas com fornecedores para ganhar eficiência.

roles.title: Sr. Principal Architect Leader
roles.arc: Setting the enterprise architecture direction for Agriculture & Trading — AI capabilities, Platform-as-a-Product, and global integration
roles.arcPt: Definindo a direção de arquitetura corporativa para Agriculture & Trading — capacidades de IA, Platform-as-a-Product e integração global

impact.narrative: Anchors the enterprise AI and GenAI capabilities for Agriculture & Trading — from agentic engineering patterns to a GenAI productivity capability for the front-office research workflow.
impact.narrativePt: Ancora as capacidades de IA e GenAI corporativa para Agriculture & Trading — de padrões de engenharia agêntica a uma capacidade de produtividade GenAI para o fluxo de pesquisa do front-office.

impact.narrative: Delivered the Future State Strategy for Data and Integration, establishing the architecture direction for a Platform-as-a-Product data estate across a highly matrixed, federated organization.
impact.narrativePt: Entregou a Future State Strategy para Dados e Integração, estabelecendo a direção de arquitetura para um portfólio de dados Platform-as-a-Product em uma organização altamente matricial e federada.

impact.narrative: Functional direction across a large, federated architecture and engineering organization spanning trading, integration, and data — Enterprise Architecture as the connective tissue for the Agriculture & Trading business.
impact.narrativePt: Direção funcional sobre uma grande organização federada de arquitetura e engenharia abrangendo trading, integração e dados — Arquitetura Corporativa como elo entre as áreas do negócio de Agriculture & Trading.

roles.title: Head of Data & Analytics
roles.arc: Building the data and analytics function and delivering significant savings across the business
roles.arcPt: Criando a função de dados e analytics e entregando economias significativas para o negócio

impact.narrative: Significantly reduced SAP programme cost and BI run-cost, demonstrating the direct business value of the new data and analytics function.
impact.narrativePt: Reduziu significativamente os custos do programa SAP e de operação de BI, demonstrando o valor direto da nova função de dados e analytics para o negócio.

impact.narrative: Built and led the data and analytics function from the ground up at a fast-scaling EV charging infrastructure company — team, tooling, and operating model designed to support rapid growth.
impact.narrativePt: Construiu e liderou a função de dados e analytics do zero em uma empresa de infraestrutura de carregamento de VE em rápido crescimento — equipe, ferramentas e modelo operacional pensados para apoiar o crescimento acelerado.

roles.title: Senior Manager, Data and SAP
roles.arc: Taking PwC's SAP advisory from its first Netherlands program to established practice
roles.arcPt: Levando o SAP advisory da PwC do primeiro programa nos Países Baixos a uma prática estabelecida

impact.narrative: Responsible for the first SAP program ever sold by PwC in the Netherlands — a utilities company digital transformation that defined the ETRM/ERP capability boundary and asset-management split across both systems.
impact.narrativePt: Responsável pelo primeiro programa SAP vendido pela PwC nos Países Baixos — uma transformação digital de empresa de utilities que definiu a fronteira de capacidades entre ETRM e ERP, incluindo a separação de gestão de ativos entre os dois sistemas.

impact.narrative: Led the Central Finance Data and R2R workstream at a major global brewer — the most complex financial data design challenge in the program.
impact.narrativePt: Liderou o workstream de Central Finance Data e R2R em uma grande cervejaria global — o desafio mais complexo de design de dados financeiros no programa.

impact.narrative: Led the inception of PwC's SAP practice in the Netherlands, establishing the delivery model and team structure from the ground up.
impact.narrativePt: Liderou a criação da prática SAP da PwC nos Países Baixos, estabelecendo o modelo de entrega e a estrutura de equipe do zero.

roles.title: Finance Consultant/Business Architect
roles.arc: Delivering best-practice SAP S/4HANA builds that materially reduce client cost
roles.arcPt: Entregando builds SAP S/4HANA de melhores práticas que reduzem materialmente os custos do cliente

impact.narrative: Business architecture on an SAP S/4HANA order-to-cash rollout: standard, best-practice build design that materially reduced client implementation cost.
impact.narrativePt: Arquitetura de negócio em um rollout de order-to-cash em SAP S/4HANA: design de build padronizado e de melhores práticas que reduziu materialmente o custo de implementação para o cliente.

roles.title: Order-to-Cash Solution Lead
roles.arc: Owning the order-to-cash solution on Siemens' Global SAP S/4HANA rollout
roles.arcPt: Liderando a solução order-to-cash no rollout global do SAP S/4HANA da Siemens

impact.narrative: Led the order-to-cash solution on the Global SAP S/4HANA rollout — responsible for process design, system configuration, and delivery across multiple markets.
impact.narrativePt: Liderou a solução order-to-cash no rollout global do SAP S/4HANA — responsável por design de processos, configuração do sistema e entrega em múltiplos mercados.

impact.narrative: Delivered a standardized, best-practice order-to-cash build that significantly reduced run-cost for the programme.
impact.narrativePt: Entregou um build order-to-cash padronizado e de melhores práticas que reduziu significativamente o custo operacional do programa.

positioning.statement: Felipe Tavares is a senior enterprise and business architecture leader with 18 years across trading, banking, consulting and manufacturing industries. The consistent thread: translating complex trade and operational processes into defensible target architectures, and, increasingly, turning AI and data strategy into measurable business value. He leads in a global matrix and has moved architecture from gatekeeping to orchestrator and hands-on delivery.

hero.headline: Enterprise architecture and AI strategy, built to survive contact with the real business.
hero.slogan: Architecting AI ambition into enterprise reality.
hero.subhead: 18 years turning Architecture, AI, Data, Platform Engineering and Product Management strategies into shipped value.

skillDomains.name: AI & Agentic Engineering
skillDomains.blurb: Built the business case for 8-figure AI and data value in commodity trading, shipping AI-augmented architecture platforms hands-on.

skillDomains.name: Enterprise & Business Architecture
skillDomains.blurb: Leads a central architecture function: authored the Architecture Guiding Principles and architect behaviour model across a federated global org.

skillDomains.name: Data Architecture & Engineering
skillDomains.blurb: Architected the future-state of an 8-figure enterprise data platform (lakehouse, streaming, API-first), now underpinning trading domains.

skillDomains.name: Cloud & Platform Architecture
skillDomains.blurb: Drives a Platform-as-a-Product operating model on AWS: golden paths, IaC and Policy as Code, a Terraform pipeline catalogue.

skillDomains.name: Integration Architecture
skillDomains.blurb: Defined the enterprise future-state event-driven, API-led integration architecture and leads its adoption across global trading programs.

skillDomains.name: Digital Strategy & IT Strategy
skillDomains.blurb: Sets technology strategy for complex, regulated domains, including a multi-vendor buy-versus-build assessment for commodity trading systems.

skillDomains.name: Data Management & Analytics
skillDomains.blurb: Delivered data products credited with a double-digit-million revenue uplift, plus multi-million-dollar cost reductions across the product-data estate.

skillDomains.name: Software & Agentic Engineering
skillDomains.blurb: Builds hands-on: an in-house AI-augmented architecture platform that auto-assembles context for delivery teams and AI coding assistants.

skillDomains.name: SAP Delivery, Strategy & Architecture
skillDomains.blurb: Led SAP S/4HANA business architecture and order-to-cash transformations, cutting the cost of a heavily customized system by half.

skillDomains.name: Portfolio & Program Management
skillDomains.blurb: Management-team member on a 9-figure cloud migration program, with functional direction across a large architecture and engineering organization.

skillDomains.name: Product Management & Agile Delivery
skillDomains.blurb: SCRUM and SAFe practitioner, CSPO and CSM certified. Brought agile delivery discipline to ERP/treasury and architecture operating models.

skillDomains.name: Technology Leadership & Org Design
skillDomains.blurb: Directly led multiple engineering, architecture and product teams, each with its own lead, and hundreds more through a federated, matrix operating model.

statements: Directly led multiple engineering, architecture and product teams, each with its own lead
Led hundreds of people across teams through a matrix operating model
18 years across trading, banking, consulting and manufacturing industries
Moved architecture from gatekeeping to orchestrator and hands-on delivery

cards.title: Mentoring
cards.body: I mentor engineers and architects working through those exact questions: how to structure a system, how to grow into the next role.

cards.title: Advisory conversations
cards.body: I sit on internal architecture and governance boards, and advise founders building in adjacent spaces. If you want a second opinion on a target architecture or an early AI bet, that's the conversation.

cards.title: Speaking & panels
cards.body: I speak on architecture, AI strategy, and technology leadership at conferences, panels, and in education settings. Specifics, not slideware.

dossier.summary: Sr. Principal Architect Leader with 18 years across trading, banking, consulting and manufacturing industries. Director-level enterprise-architecture leadership, leading a central architecture function with functional direction over a large architecture and engineering organization (typically ≈10 direct, 20+ dotted-line). Leads enterprise and business architecture, AI, and data strategy across a global Fortune 500 organization's Agriculture and Trading and Data and Integration portfolios, including enterprise CTRM strategy. Built the business case attributing 8-figure value to AI and data initiatives, and architected the future-state of an 8-figure enterprise data platform. Moves architecture from gatekeeping to orchestrator and hands-on delivery.

competencyMatrix.evidence: Architecture Guiding Principles, architect behaviour model, and global architecture governance process across a federated organization.
Defined the enterprise future-state event-driven, API-led integration architecture and leads its adoption across global trading programs.
8-figure AI/data value business case; built an in-house AI-augmented architecture platform (event-driven, CQRS, hexagonal) hands-on.
Architected the future-state of an 8-figure enterprise data platform (lakehouse, streaming, API-first consumption), plus MDM/data governance architecture.
Drives a Platform-as-a-Product operating model on AWS: golden paths, IaC and Policy as Code, a Terraform pipeline catalogue.
Multi-vendor buy-versus-build assessment and hybrid target architecture for global commodity trading systems; commodities technology strategy and roadmap.
Delivered data products credited with a double-digit-million revenue uplift, plus multi-million-dollar cost reductions across the product-data estate.
Builds hands-on: an in-house AI-augmented architecture platform that auto-assembles a queryable context layer for delivery teams and AI coding assistants.
Led SAP S/4HANA business architecture and order-to-cash transformations, including a rollout that cut maintenance cost of a customized system by more than half.
Management-team member on a 9-figure cloud migration program; functional direction across a large architecture and engineering organization spanning trading, integration, and data.
CSPO & CSM certified; SCRUM/SAFe delivery leadership.
Directly led multiple engineering, architecture and product teams, each with its own lead, and hundreds of people across teams through a matrix operating model.

education.school: Universidade Federal do Estado do Rio de Janeiro (UNIRIO)

decisions.title: Astro over Next.js
decisions.context: The site is mostly static, SEO-critical content read by recruiters and peers on commutes, not an app that needs client-side routing state.
decisions.decision: Astro 5 with React islands only where interactivity is real (chat, forms, theme toggle), instead of Next.js.
decisions.cost: Gave up Next's larger ecosystem and app-router conveniences. Bought near-zero client JS on every content page by default — a budget Next would need deliberate work to hit, since its defaults ship a client-first runtime whether a page needs one or not.

decisions.title: Git-based CMS over a hosted one
decisions.context: No evidence yet that a plain git-plus-editor workflow can't keep up with actual publishing cadence, before paying for a hosted CMS to solve a problem that might not exist.
decisions.decision: Content lives as git-versioned MDX/JSON, read through one ContentRepository port, edited three equivalent ways — direct file, Claude Code, or a local Keystatic UI.
decisions.cost: Gave up a hosted UI's polish and multi-editor collaboration. Bought a $0 CMS with the swap to Sanity or Payload staying a single new adapter file, not a page-level rewrite, if the workflow ever does hit a ceiling.

decisions.title: Workers AI first, Anthropic as fallback
decisions.context: The chatbot needs a model provider, and a paid API as the default breaks the $0/month budget the moment the site gets any real traffic.
decisions.decision: A provider router tries Cloudflare Workers AI first, free and colocated with the rest of the stack, and falls back to Anthropic only if that call errors — never the other way around.
decisions.cost: Workers AI quality trails frontier models on nuanced questions. The tradeoff: a chatbot that costs nothing at normal traffic, with the upgrade to Claude Haiku sitting behind one config change in src/config/llm.ts, not a rewrite.

decisions.title: Cloudflare's free tier as the ceiling, not a starting point
decisions.context: Domain already owned; every other cost (hosting, embeddings, rate-limit storage, email) had to fit inside a tier that won't get bumped by a personal site's real traffic, not just look cheap on a demo.
decisions.decision: Pages/Workers for hosting, KV for rate limiting, Vectorize for embeddings, Workers AI for the chatbot — one provider, one free tier, for everything that isn't the domain itself.
decisions.cost: Gave up a multi-vendor stack that might optimize any one piece slightly better. Bought a $0/month recurring bill regardless of how many features get added, because they all share the same headroom instead of each adding its own bill.

decisions.title: The hero moved from decorative to data-true
decisions.context: An early hero concept was a generic animated gradient mesh with an optional pointer-parallax dot grid: motion for its own sake, no connection to what the site is actually about.
decisions.decision: Replaced it with a living-blueprint diagram generated at build time from the real competency matrix — nodes are skill domains weighted by real depth, edges are roles that touched them. Not decorative randomness.
decisions.cost: More build complexity than a static gradient. The hero now changes only when the underlying career data changes — the diagram is a claim about real experience, not a mood board.

decisions.title: A hand-written fetch transport over the MCP SDK's own
decisions.context: The MCP server's Cloudflare Worker transport needed to implement the protocol's Transport interface, and the SDK ships one, StreamableHTTPServerTransport, for exactly this.
decisions.decision: Traced that class into node_modules and found it's typed and implemented directly against Node's IncomingMessage/ServerResponse, which don't exist in the Workers runtime. Wrote FetchTransport instead: the same interface, implemented directly over the standard Fetch API.
decisions.cost: Gave up the SDK's official, more-tested transport implementation. Bought a Worker that actually runs, verified with a real `wrangler dev` handshake, instead of one that fails at runtime reaching for a Node built-in that isn't there.

stack.name: Astro 5
stack.reason: Islands architecture ships near-zero client JS on static content by default.

stack.name: React (islands only)
stack.reason: Used only where a component genuinely needs client-side state — chat, forms, toggles.

stack.name: TypeScript, hexagonal ports & adapters
stack.reason: core stays framework-free; swapping an adapter never touches a page.

stack.name: Tailwind CSS v4
stack.reason: Token-driven styling; every color and spacing value traces to one CSS custom-property file.

stack.name: Cloudflare Pages + Workers
stack.reason: Hybrid output: pages prerender to the CDN, /api/* runs as Workers functions.

stack.name: Cloudflare Workers AI + Vectorize
stack.reason: Free-tier chatbot provider and embedding index, with Anthropic as a config-swappable fallback.

stack.name: Cloudflare KV
stack.reason: Rate-limit storage for /api/contact and /api/chat, free tier, no separate database.

stack.name: Resend
stack.reason: Contact form email delivery via the REST API; the RESEND_API_KEY env binding keeps it out of the bundle.

stack.name: Cloudflare Turnstile
stack.reason: Server-side bot verification on /api/contact; sitekey is public, secret stays in the Worker env binding.

stack.name: Astro content collections (git-based CMS)
stack.reason: content/ is the CMS — MDX/JSON, versioned in git, edited three equivalent ways.

stack.name: Keystatic
stack.reason: Local content-editing UI mirroring the same zod schemas Astro's collections already validate.

stack.name: GSAP, Lenis, OGL (motion stack)
stack.reason: MIT-licensed; loaded lazily per page so content pages never pay for motion they don't use.

stack.name: Model Context Protocol (packages/mcp)
stack.reason: Publishes the same content and chatbot to AI agents as typed tools, not scraped HTML.

stack.name: Vitest + Playwright
stack.reason: Vitest for unit and architecture boundary tests; Playwright for cross-engine E2E across Chromium, WebKit, and mobile-WebKit.

changelog.title: Experience and career narrative published
changelog.detail: Scroll-driven career timeline and printable executive dossier go live, covering seven roles.

changelog.title: Projects, writing engine, and chatbot launch
changelog.detail: Project deep-dives, an RSS-backed writing engine with per-article publish kits, and a RAG chatbot built on Cloudflare Workers AI ship in one push.

changelog.title: Accessibility controls added
changelog.detail: A floating panel ships with reduce-motion, high-contrast, and larger-text toggles, backed by a published accessibility statement.

changelog.title: AI-readable surfaces ship
changelog.detail: Markdown twins for every page, an llms.txt file, and a JSON Resume endpoint go live. An AI agent can read this site cleanly without scraping HTML.

changelog.title: MCP server live at mcp.felipetavares.dev
changelog.detail: Career data and the chatbot are exposed as typed Model Context Protocol tools. Any MCP-capable agent can query roles, search articles, or start a grounded chat.

changelog.title: Brand identity and galaxy backdrop ship
changelog.detail: The owner's brand glyph replaces placeholder marks across favicon, OG image, and chat launcher. A WebGL galaxy layer sits behind all page content, tuned for light and dark themes.

changelog.title: /reading section opens
changelog.detail: A public log of books, articles, and papers, each with a verdict and rationale. Filterable by type and domain, with four verdict levels.

changelog.title: Site deployed to Cloudflare Pages at $0/month
changelog.detail: The full stack (Pages, Workers, KV, Vectorize, Workers AI) runs inside Cloudflare's free tier. Total recurring cost after the domain: $0/month.

changelog.title: Structured data and SEO pass
changelog.detail: Person, WebSite, ProfilePage, and Article JSON-LD added across all main pages. Desktop Lighthouse scores after the pass: 100/100/100/100.

changelog.title: Agent-discovery endpoints added
changelog.detail: A well-known API catalog, an MCP server card, and HTTP Link headers on the homepage let AI agents find the site's machine-readable surfaces without guessing paths.
